response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've always been a bit skeptical about social media, especially since I'm not online all the time like some younger folks. At my age, you learn that not everything is what it seems, but seeing it firsthand can be tough.

Dealing with fake content feels much like dealing with people in life who are less than truthful. As a waiter and now self-employed, I've seen plenty of dishonesty—short-changing customers or misrepresenting dishes, for instance—and that's not even on the digital stage. Social media is different because it affects everyone so quickly.

I think managing this starts with knowing when to look closely. Like reading the fine print before signing anything important. My depression makes me sometimes want to just scroll and forget about it all, but that would be like ignoring reality at the worst times. You have to engage your brain a bit more than usual.

One thing I do is check multiple sources for any news or viral posts—kind of how when I worked as a waiter, I'd ask other servers if they saw something odd with an order before serving it blindly.

Also, my hobbies help: yoga keeps me calm so I don't react too harshly; cycling clears my head. But maybe what's most important is to set boundaries on time spent online. I only check a few times weekly anyway, and that feels more manageable.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on the persona traits—being older with life experience (especially from years in service roles), dealing with depression which requires careful emotional management, high extraversion meaning enjoying social interaction but also needing to balance it—I drew parallels between real-world experiences of dishonesty and online fake content. I suggested using caution strategies similar to those learned through work or daily interactions.
response_parsed.tone: Calm yet reflective, with a touch of wisdom from experience interspersed with personal struggles.
